text: The surgeon begins the initial dissection by developing the left retro-rectus space inferiorly past the arcuate line towards Cooper's ligament. Under direct vision, safe port placement is achieved. The dissection continues medially towards the hernia defect at the linea alba using monopolar cautery, sharp, and blunt dissection techniques. Sharp dissection is used to incise the linea alba below its decussations at the midline. In the mid-abdomen, the peritoneum is incised inferiorly, revealing the Swiss cheese hernia defect, and the contents are reduced.

The surgeon continues dissection inferiorly at the arcuate line within the preperitoneal space, dropping the preperitoneal flap from the midline. The dissection progresses cranially towards the falciform ligament and retroxiphoid fat pad, entering the preperitoneal space. The contralateral rectus sheath is incised and dissected to expose the retrorectus space.

The surgeon connects the inferior preperitoneal flap to the contralateral flap and continues dissection of the right retro-rectus space up to the linea semilunaris, ensuring preservation of the neurovascular bundles. The posterior flaps are evaluated, and the hernia defect is measured. The largest supraumbilical defect measures four by four centimeters, while the total defect length is twelve centimeters. The midline defect is closed with a running O permanent suture.

The surgeon closes the posterior flap using a 2-0 semi-permanent suture in a running modified canal stitch fashion. A mid-weight polypropylene mesh, trimmed to 14 by 25 centimeters, is introduced into the retro-rectus space, unrolled, and laid flat. Twenty milliliters of fibrin sealant is applied for fixation and hemostasis.
